questions.question: What is the core distinction between a forward proxy and a reverse proxy?
options: Forward proxies are faster than reverse proxies
A forward proxy acts on behalf of clients; a reverse proxy acts on behalf of servers
Forward proxies use TCP; reverse proxies use UDP
There is no real difference; the terms are interchangeable
questions.explanation: The defining difference is which side they represent. A forward proxy sits in front of clients (the server sees the proxy's IP). A reverse proxy sits in front of servers (the client sees one address hiding a pool of backends).

questions.question: Why terminate TLS at a reverse proxy rather than at each backend?
options: TLS cannot run on application servers
One certificate to manage and the crypto work happens once, so backends speak plain HTTP on the trusted internal network
It makes the connection less secure on purpose
Backends are required by law to avoid encryption
questions.explanation: Terminating TLS at the proxy means one certificate to rotate instead of one per server, and the expensive encryption/decryption happens at the edge. Backends then receive plain HTTP over a trusted internal network, saving CPU.

questions.question: Which capability is characteristic of an API gateway but beyond a plain load balancer?
options: Forwarding TCP packets to a backend
Centralized authentication, rate limiting, and request aggregation across different services
Having an IP address
Running on Linux
questions.explanation: A load balancer's core job is spreading traffic across identical backends. An API gateway adds application-level policy: authenticating once at the edge, enforcing rate limits, routing by API version, and aggregating multiple service calls into one response.

questions.question: When does an API gateway provide the most value?
options: When you have a single monolithic service
When you have many microservices that would otherwise each re-implement auth, rate limiting, and logging
Only when traffic is below 10 requests per second
Never; reverse proxies always suffice
questions.explanation: The gateway pattern shines with microservices. Instead of every service duplicating cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, observability), the gateway handles them once at the edge, letting each service focus on its domain. For a single service, a plain reverse proxy is usually enough.

questions.question: What is 'request aggregation' at an API gateway?
options: Compressing requests to save bandwidth
The gateway fans one client request out to several backend services and merges their responses
Storing requests in a queue for later
Rejecting requests that exceed a rate limit
questions.explanation: Request aggregation lets a client (e.g. a mobile app) make one call that the gateway expands into several backend calls, combining the results into a single response. This reduces round trips for the client and keeps orchestration logic out of the clients.
